questions.text: What is the time complexity of git bisect for finding a bad commit among N commits?

options.text: O(n) — must test every commit in the worst case
O(n²) — comparisons grow quadratically
O(log n) — each step halves the remaining search space
O(1) — Git can identify the bad commit without any testing

questions.explanation: git bisect uses binary search: it picks the midpoint commit between the known-good and known-bad boundaries, then halves the remaining range after each test. For 1,024 commits, the maximum number of steps is log₂(1024) = 10. This makes bisect extremely efficient even for repos with thousands of commits.

questions.text: How do you automate git bisect so it runs without manual good/bad input?

options.text: git bisect auto --script=./test.sh
git bisect run ./test-script.sh — the script exit code (0=good, non-zero=bad) marks each commit automatically
git bisect --no-interactive ./test-script.sh
Set the BISECT_SCRIPT environment variable before starting

questions.explanation: git bisect run <script> automates the process. After each checkout, Git runs the provided script. An exit code of 0 means the commit is good; exit code 1–127 (except 125) means bad; 125 means the commit cannot be tested (skip). Git continues automatically until the culprit is identified.

questions.text: What does git reflog record that `git log` does not show?

options.text: The full diff of every file changed in each commit
Every position HEAD has occupied, including checkouts, resets, rebases, and merges — not just commits
Remote push and fetch operations to origin
The author's email address for every commit in the history

questions.explanation: git log only shows commits reachable from the current branch. git reflog records every movement of HEAD, including: commits, merges, rebases (each step), resets, and checkouts. This makes it possible to recover commits that are no longer reachable from any branch, such as commits made during a rebase that was later aborted or a branch that was deleted.

questions.text: How long are reflog entries retained by default before Git expires them?

options.text: 7 days
30 days
90 days
365 days

questions.explanation: By default, git gc expires reflog entries that are older than 90 days (gc.reflogExpire = 90 days). Unreachable commits (commits not pointed to by any ref) expire after 30 days by default (gc.reflogExpireUnreachable = 30 days). These values can be adjusted in git config.

questions.text: Why is the reflog not available after cloning a repository?

options.text: git clone requires the --include-reflog flag to transfer reflog data
Reflog is a local-only record of HEAD movements; it is never pushed to remotes or included in clones
The reflog is stored in the remote's .git/reflog directory which clone ignores
Reflog is disabled by default and must be enabled with git config core.reflog true

questions.explanation: The reflog is strictly local. It records what happened in your working copy of the repository — operations you personally performed. When you clone, you get a fresh copy with no history of local operations, so the reflog starts empty. It is not part of the transfer protocol and cannot be pushed or fetched.

questions.text: What is the key advantage of `git worktree` over cloning a repository for working on multiple branches?

options.text: Worktrees allow branches to share the same staging area (index)
Worktrees share the same .git object store, so no history or objects are duplicated — faster and uses far less disk space
Worktrees automatically sync commits between branches in real time
Worktrees can check out branches from multiple different remote repositories simultaneously

questions.explanation: A git worktree adds a new working directory linked to the same .git directory. All worktrees share the same object store, refs, and config — no duplication of the repository history. In contrast, cloning creates a completely separate copy of all objects. For large repos, a worktree is significantly faster to create and uses much less disk space.

questions.text: What does the `.gitmodules` file in a repository contain?

options.text: The list of ignored paths for all submodule directories
The mapping of submodule paths to their remote URLs, along with optional config like branch and shallow settings
The pinned SHA-1 of each submodule's checked-out commit
The Git version required by each submodule

questions.explanation: .gitmodules is a text configuration file that records the path and URL of each submodule. Example entry: [submodule "vendor/lib"] path = vendor/lib, url = https://github.com/org/lib.git. The pinned commit SHA is stored in the parent repo's tree/index as a special 'gitlink' entry, not in .gitmodules.

questions.text: How do you clone a repository and immediately get all submodule content populated?

options.text: git clone --with-submodules URL
git clone URL && git submodule fetch --all
git clone --recurse-submodules URL
git clone URL && git submodule sync

questions.explanation: git clone --recurse-submodules URL clones the parent repository and immediately initializes and clones all submodules (recursively, including nested submodules). Without this flag, submodule directories exist but are empty after cloning. You can achieve the same result on an already-cloned repo with: git submodule update --init --recursive.

options.text: That all commits in the bundle have valid GPG signatures
That the bundle file is not corrupted and that the receiving repository has the prerequisite commits the bundle depends on
That the bundle was created from the same repository as the target
That no large binary files are included in the bundle

questions.explanation: git bundle verify confirms that: (1) the bundle file header and data are structurally valid and not corrupted, and (2) if the bundle is incremental (created with a prerequisite like ^v2.0.0), the target repository already contains the prerequisite objects. If prerequisites are missing, applying the bundle would fail — verify catches this upfront.

questions.text: You accidentally deleted a branch with `git branch -D feature/login`. How do you recover it using the reflog?

options.text: git checkout feature/login — Git automatically restores deleted branches
git branch feature/login origin/feature/login — fetch from the remote
Run git reflog to find the tip SHA of the deleted branch, then git checkout -b feature/login SHA to recreate it
git branch --restore feature/login — the built-in restore command

questions.explanation: When a branch is deleted, its commits remain in the object store (until garbage collected). Run git reflog to see past HEAD positions — you'll find the SHA of the last commit on the deleted branch (look for 'commit: <message>' entries or 'checkout: moving to feature/login'). Then git checkout -b feature/login SHA recreates the branch pointer at that commit.
